original_abstract: Autoregressive decoding in Transformer models is frequently bottlenecked by the memory bandwidth required to load key-value caches. While multi-query attention (MQA) alleviates this overhead by using a single key-value head, it often results in reduced model quality and necessitates costly training from scratch. In this paper, we present two primary contributions to improve inference efficiency. First, we introduce a method for "uptraining" existing multi-head attention (MHA) checkpoints into MQA models, achieving high performance with only 5% of the original pre-training compute. Second, we propose grouped-query attention (GQA), a generalized approach that uses an intermediate number of key-value heads. Our results demonstrate that GQA effectively interpolates between MHA and MQA, delivering model quality close to MHA while maintaining inference speeds comparable to MQA. These methods provide a resource-efficient path toward deploying high-capacity LLMs with significantly reduced latency.
humanized_abstract: Autoregressive decoding in Transformer models is frequently constrained by memory bandwidth limitations due to key-value cache loading. Multi-query attention (MQA) has been proposed to alleviate such constraints using a single key-value head. However, its corresponding model performance is generally lower than that of the original Transformer model and requires full from-scratch learning over millions of parameters to obtain small losses, which is computationally expensive. In this work, we show that we can - Up-train an MHA model into an MQA model with very few training epochs (using just 5% of the original pre-training compute), - Generalize multi-head attention (MHA) and multi-query attention (MQA) into a new variant called Grouped-Query Attention (GQA), which supports a wide range of key-value head counts. As such, we find that, by tuning a few hyperparameters, our GQA approach can achieve similar performance to MHA, yet faster inference time than MQA. These results contribute to the development of scalable, low-latency, large-language-models (LLMs).

As such, we find that, by tuning a few hyperparameters, our GQA approach can achieve similar performance to MHA, yet faster inference time than MQA.
